title: Weather and meteorology glossary - Canada.ca
content:
## Weather and meteorology glossary
In this glossary, you'll find terms used in our public forecasting program and weather observations, and unique terminology related to Environment and Climate Change Canada's hurricane centre and ice service products.

The humidex is a tool we use to describe how hot, humid weather feels on your skin. It combines the temperature and humidity into one number, and it can be a better measure of how hot the air feels than temperature or humidity alone. The hot, humid air masses, which cause such uncomfortable weather, usually come from the Gulf of Mexico or the Caribbean.
How to use the humidex as a guide:
Range of humidex: degree of comfort
- 20 to 29 °C: little discomfort
- 30 to 39 °C: some discomfort
- 40 to 45 °C: great discomfort; avoid exertion
- above 45 °C: dangerous; heat stroke possible
Any value over 40 °C is an extremely high humidex reading. At that point, you should reduce all unnecessary physical activity.
For a humidex reading above 30 °C, you should limit or modify outdoor exercise, depending on:
- individual physical limitations
- the type of clothes you are wearing, and
- other weather conditions
Extremely high readings on the humidex are rare except in the southern regions of Ontario, Manitoba, and Quebec. Learn how to calculate the humidex in your region with the humidex calculator.

A type of alert from Environment and Climate Change Canada's Meteorological Service (MSC) where:
- A warning or watch is not warranted because the expected conditions are less menacing than those of a warning, or
- A warning or watch is not yet practical to issue because of the high degree of uncertainty of occurrence, location and/or timing of the possible hazardous condition(s)
A special weather statement can be used to describe any hazard (unlike an advisory). The statements do not have a formal "in effect" status, since there is no requirement to update or end them.
